teamObjective.explanation: As the opening leader with a weak non-trump hand, I want to start cheaply so the team can contest or win the trick without committing high cards.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner has 3 unseen cards and no public signal yet; they may hold a mid/high card or a trump to take this trick if opponents do not overtake it.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents each hold 3 unseen cards. Given the tiny sample of public history, they likely possess some higher non-trump cards or trumps that could beat a low spade lead, but no specific card can be asserted.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of spades discards the weakest card, keeps the slightly stronger 4-6 for future tricks, and avoids revealing or wasting high-card strength. A low lead minimizes commitment while still allowing partner a chance to take the trick.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 4 is higher than the 3 in the same suit; playing it now wastes incremental strength without improving the chance of winning the trick.
The 5 is even more valuable for later play in a low hand; a low lead achieves the same information/control goals.
The 6 is the highest non-trump card in hand and the best hope for future control; leading it risks losing our strongest card to a routine trump or higher card.

risks: The low lead is unlikely to win outright if any opponent has a higher non-trump or a trump.
Partner may be unable to capture the trick, ceding lead to an opponent.
Retaining 4-6 may not be enough to regain control later if opponents hold trumps.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Keep all remaining cards, especially the 6, and avoid playing trumps or bombs until the hand distribution becomes clearer.
